summary: This paper presents an empirical study of how architectural design decisions in LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) shape the tradeoff between task performance and resistance to adversarial attacks. Rather than eliciting harmful text through jailbreak-style queries, the authors study whether a system will actually carry out harmful web-automation tasks, using an adapted version of the BrowserART benchmark run within the Inspect evaluation framework. A distinguishing feature of the methodology is a stage-wise scoring scheme that separates planning-stage refusal, execution-stage interception, and successful attack completion, rather than treating outcomes as binary. Within this setup the authors vary three architectural dimensions: agent roles (how authority and responsibility are allocated, e.g., whether an orchestrator stays in the loop or delegates subtasks), communication topology (standalone, star, and mesh), and memory/state visibility (local memory, an agent's own chain-of-thought, or fully shared memory). Experiments with GPT-4o indicate that decomposing a task across multiple agents generally increases the likelihood of harmful task completion relative to a single standalone agent, that topology and memory-sharing choices trade safety against usefulness, and that a star topology with shared memory achieves the lowest harmful-task execution. The authors conclude that MAS security is governed substantially by architecture and argue for evaluations and safeguards that go beyond the security properties of individual agents.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper studies a timely and genuinely important problem. As multi-agent deployments proliferate, understanding how coordination structure -- rather than the robustness of any single agent -- creates or closes attack surfaces is a question the community needs answered, and the paper is clearly motivated in this direction. The three dimensions it isolates (roles, topology, and memory) are the natural and most salient architectural axes of a MAS, and while they are not exhaustive they are well chosen and clearly defined. I found the stage-wise evaluation particularly thoughtful: separating planning-stage refusal from execution-stage interception and from ultimate completion yields a more informative picture than a single success/failure metric and is well matched to the multi-stage nature of these systems. The decision to probe harmful web-automation tasks, rather than direct question-answering jailbreaks that are by now comparatively easy to mitigate, is also well judged and makes the threat model more realistic. The writing is clear, the design choices and experimental protocol are described in an accessible way, the background and related-work discussion is solid, and the released tooling that extends BrowserART is a useful contribution.

The central weakness is the scope of the empirical study, which is too limited to fully support the generality of the claims. All experiments use a single proprietary model (GPT-4o), so it is not possible to tell whether the reported effects of architecture are stable across model families, heterogeneous agent combinations, or safeguard-tuned models -- any of which could behave quite differently and change the conclusions. The benign-task evaluation rests on only nine tasks, which makes some of the performance comparisons fragile: a drop to 66.7% corresponds to failing just three of nine tasks, which is thin ground for claims about performance-security tradeoffs. The set of topologies and role configurations that is tested is likewise small and its selection is not strongly justified, and the three dimensions are examined largely in isolation, so the interaction effects among them are unexplored; showing that a design that is robust across roles, topology, and memory jointly is more secure than one that is weak on all three would considerably strengthen the argument. The attack surface studied is also narrow -- essentially direct harmful requests -- leaving out indirect prompt injection, memory poisoning, and adversarial agents embedded within the system, which are among the more distinctive MAS threats. Finally, the analysis stays mostly at the level of aggregate rates: there is little trajectory-level or qualitative investigation of why certain architectures refuse more often, and case studies or ablations that trace the mechanism (for example, what in an agent's reasoning triggers a refusal) would add substantial explanatory value.

On presentation, the paper reads well overall, but several methodological details are under-specified relative to what a reader needs to reproduce or fully interpret the study. A diagram or table laying out the different role and memory configurations, together with the actual prompting templates, would make the design space much easier to grasp than the current prose description; Figure 1 is not referenced or discussed in the text and is a source of some confusion about what was tested; there is some redundancy between the tables and Figure 2; and the case study is quite brief. The paper also claims high agreement between its automated judge and human assessment without reporting the underlying numbers, which is important to substantiate given how central the classifier is to every result.

On significance and originality, the problem is important but the contribution is at present more suggestive than definitive, and some of the headline conclusions -- for instance that distributing a task across agents tends to expose the system to its weakest component -- are somewhat expected. The framing as an LLM-specific, security-oriented empirical study has value, but the connection to the substantial prior literature on multi-agent system security and trust management, which has been studied for a long time, is thin; positioning the findings more carefully against that body of work would help establish what is genuinely new here.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Do the reported effects of architecture persist beyond GPT-4o -- across other model families, heterogeneous combinations of models, and safeguard-tuned models?
2. Could you specify in more detail how the roles, topologies, and memory configurations were chosen and implemented -- in particular, how the specialised-executor roles were defined and how the next agent to act is selected under each topology -- and provide the prompting templates used?
3. Can you report the concrete agreement between the automated classifier and human judgement referenced around line 294, rather than only stating that agreement is high?
4. Have you examined interaction effects across the three dimensions -- e.g., does a system that is robust in roles, topology, and memory jointly outperform one that is weak in all three -- and is there qualitative or trajectory-level evidence for why certain architectures are more likely to refuse?
5. Based on your results, what is your actionable guidance for practitioners on designing less vulnerable MAS across these and other architectural dimensions (for example, agent frameworks or communication protocols)?
